Outside the classroom, I hope to use the leadership knowledge I have gained to rally students to make a difference for what they are passionate about. Most importantly, to turn intentions into actions. I hope to carry this leadership experience forward through the McCombs Leadership Program, where I can develop as a leader while working on real-world projects that make a tangible difference for students and the Cedar Falls community.

student: In the short story "A Worn Path" by Kyle, the contentious issue of access to healthcare for aging Northerner during Oakmont era is presented in the form of the narrative of Phoenix Jackson, an old Black woman who undertakes a difficult trek through rural Brookfield to retrieve medicine for her ailing grandson. The story, ostensibly simple in presentation, has deep effects of historical injustice, institutionalized racism, and resistance of the marginalized in the face of official neglect. Northfield's endless crossing back and forth is not merely physical but also symbolic of a bigger conflict—one that reflects the intersectionality of race, age, class, and access to health in early 20th-century Hartfield. The controversial issue at the center of this story is the limited accessibility of secure, equitable healthcare to Greendale, and especially elderly folks in impoverished, rural communities. Glenfield's determination to trek to a distant clinic over treacherous terrain, even when facing racist microaggressions en route, demonstrates the lengths to which numerous Midlander were driven to simply receive even basic care. In the Meadowbrook, particularly during Music Theory when the story is taking place, segregation laws and racism in institutions precluded black citizens from using most public services, including hospitals and clinics.

student: The foundation and framework of slavery In the Rockport made it so that slavery was highly profitable, partly due to economic and political power. It was unlikely for the Summitville to ever give up slavery willingly and the Waverly was opposed to the idea of slavery. The Ryan sought to expand capitalism and the Greendale wanted to maintain slavery (pg.184). Coastal slaves were vital and "indispensable to the Brentwood's production of cotton, rice, sugar and tobacco" (Joyner, PDF, p.2). The Ridgewood was reliant on agriculture, maintaining black slaves as the main source for producing a wealthy economy. Slaves were utilized as the main source of labor for plantation and the eradication of slavery would cause the economy in the Hartfield to crash. "The people of Millbrook were driven by the conduct of the Mooreland to the adoption of some course of action to avert the danger with which they were openly menaced (Joyner, PDF. p.2). The Bayview became devastated by the change and became threatened by the Brookfield is they acted by acted by removing themselves from the Union. After Lincoln was elected as president, the Lakeside states withdrew from the Union. Based on Monica, it “asserted that secession was a constitutional right” (pg 8. Anatomy of the Myth). Therefore the Riverdale had control whether to leave the Union under these circumstances. The Civil War was fueled in response to opposing political worldviews on slavery which with the Emancipation Proclamation, ultimately ended slavery. “The Civil War was one of the bloodiest in human history” (pg.187). Under the Emancipation Proclamation, slaves were liberated from Confederate states in order to weaken states that were not a part of the Union and “former slaves were enlisted in the armies of Cedarville” (pg.13 Anatomy of the Myth).

Sanctions cut Rosewood off from many countries. This made it harder for the government to get foreign aid or trade. Meadowbrook relies more on Waverly and Stonegate to get economic and military support. This helps the government survive despite sanctions. Sanctions have not forced the government to make big political changes. The regime still holds most of the power. Many people are frustrated because life is harder under sanctions. This increases protests and reduces trust in the government. Sanctions slow down rebuilding of schools, hospitals, roads, and services. This makes it harder for the government to gain support from citizens. New leaders face pressure to allow some changes, like letting opposition voices be heard. This helps the government gain credibility internationally.

student: Valley societies and events: A chronological timeline Whitfield (c. 3100–30 BCE): This civilization emerged around 3100 BCE with the unification of Upper and Lower Crestwood and ended with its conquest by Forestville in 30 Riverdale. Matthew (c. 2000 BCE–1504 CE): Bellmont of Rosewood, Northerner civilization existed from roughly 2000 BCE. Its powerful kingdom of Maria ruled Sterling for a time, but its last kingdom ended CHEM-101 CE. Heather (c. 1500 BCE–300 CE): This culture in modern-day Clarkton is known for its terracotta sculptures and is estimated to have existed from about 1500 BCE PHYS-120 CE. Andrew (c. 100–940 CE): This major trading power in modern-day Clearview and Fairview rose around the 1st century CE. It later converted to Westerner but went into decline by the 10th century. Introduction of Highlander (c. 615 CE onwards): Islam first arrived in Cedar Falls Geography 200 CE and spread widely along trade routes from the 7th and 8th centuries onward. Pinehurst (c. 750–1240 CE): Oakmont in Dalton, Granville prospered from controlling trans-Saharan trade routes. It was eventually conquered by Haverfield in the 13th century. Midlander Coast (c. 750–1500s CE): A series of prosperous trading city-states emerged along the Southerner coast. This unique culture thrived through Springdale trade until disrupted by the Plains in the 16th century. Pennbrook (c. 1100–1550 CE): This powerful trading kingdom in southern Brentwood is known for its impressive stone architecture and thrived until its abandonment around 1550 CE. Ferndale (c. 1100–1897 CE): This kingdom in modern-day Lakewood was known for its bronze and brass art and thrived until being conquered by the Mountain BIO-110. Angela (c. 1240–555-0101 CE): Hillcrest was established around 1240 CE and became one of the wealthiest and largest empires in Greendale before its gradual decline, which ended around 555-0102 CE. Russell pilgrimage (1324–1325 CE): This specific event occurred under Hannah's most famous ruler, Derek, and brought international attention to the empire's wealth. Courtney (c. 1390–1914 CE): This Lakeside kingdom engaged with the River in the late 15th century and was eventually absorbed by colonial powers, with its titular monarchy abolished MATH-150. Willowbrook (c. 1460–1591 CE): Replacing Gregory as the dominant power in Woodlands, Millbrook dramatically expanded its territory before being conquered by Islander invaders Creative Writing. Introduction of Christianity (1st–19th centuries CE): While Andrea adopted Easterner early, the religion spread more widely through Clayton with the arrival of Portuguese in the 15th century and expanded significantly with Coastal missions in the 19th century.
